title: practical english usage - Part 338
content:
Do we need to reserve seats on the train?

Everybody needs to rest sometimes.

by an infinitive with to.

and questions and negatives are made with do. Need is usually followed Need most often has ordinary verb forms: the third person singular has -s, 1  ordinary       verb:   Everybody          needs       to   rest.

For -ing forms after to,                   For the difference between nearest and next, / came very near (to) hitting him.

Near (to) can be followed by an -ing form.

She was near (to) despair.

We live near (to) the station. I put my bag down near (to) the door.

meaning, but is less common.

Near can be used as a preposition. Near to is also possible with the same 531 near     (to)  _ 327.

For more about the use of names, ► since in some cultures it is common to give the family name first.

Note that the terms first name and last name can sometimes cause confusion, name'. British people use middle names less often.

Walter, F. Scott Fitzgerald. The  second of these is generally called the ‘ middle Americans often use two given names, or one and an initial (e.g. Philip C.

(The expression Christian name is generally avoided in multi-cultural contexts.) are given at birth - for example Jessica, George Thomas, Alice Catherine.

First names, Christian names or given names (AmE) are the parts which  children typically passed from parents to children - for example Barlow, Shakespeare.

The  surname, family name or last name is the part which, in many cultures, is Word  Problems from A  to Z      Section 31 vocabulary •  532  need used to say that there is an obligation not to do something.

Need not or do not need to is used to say that there is no obligation; must not is 7 need      not    and   must      not I need never have packed all that suncream - it rained every day.

Need never have.. . is a more emphatic version of need not have.. .

It started raining so I didn't need to water the flowers.

I needn't have watered the flowers. Just after I finished it started raining.

are simply saying that it was not necessary (whether or not it was done).

On  the other hand, if we  say that somebody did not need to do something, we / needn't have cooked so much  food. Nobody was hungry.

You needn't have woken me up. I don't have to go to work today.

did it, but that it was unnecessary - a waste of time.

If we  say that somebody need not have done something, we  mean that he or she 6 need      not    have     .. .

You need your head examining. (BrE) (   ... examined.) A  structure with object + .. .ing or past participle is also possible in some cases.

That sofa needs cleaning again. (= ... needs to be cleaned . ..) infinitive, especially in British English.

After need an -ing form can be used with the same meaning as a passive 5 need     .. .ing:   The     sofa     needs       cleaning.

For similar uses of have to, ►     71.

You'll need to fill in this form before you see the Inspector.

You'll need to start work soon if you want to pass your exams.

We'll need to repair the roof next year.

the future. It can make orders and instructions sound less direct.

Will need to... can be used to talk about future obligation, and give advice for Tell her she doesn't need to work tonight.

Need I come in tomorrow?

Present tense forms of need are used when making decisions about the future.

4 talking     about     the   future nd I        ɪ   Yint-needn't-pay-. . .-inmost-countries.) You don't need to pay for emergency calls in most countries.

It's OK  - you needn't / don't need to pay for that phone call.

something. They  are not used to talk about habitual, general necessity.

often used to ask for or give permission - usually permission not to do These modal forms of need normally refer to immediate necessity. They  are immediate  necessity 3 needn't,       need      I?, don't have to are preferred.
